title: A bill to remove the lesser prairie-chicken from the lists of threatened species and endangered species published pursuant to the Endangered Species Act of 1973, to amend that Act to exclude the lesser prairie-chicken from the authority of that Act, and for other purposes.
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.
plain_summary: This bill removes the lesser prairie-chicken bird from the government's list of animals that need special protection because they might disappear forever. The bill also makes a new rule that says this bird can never be added back to the protection list in the future. This means the government cannot use the Endangered Species Act to protect lesser prairie-chickens or their homes. The bill applies to all groups of lesser prairie-chickens, no matter where they live.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

sections.title: Section 1  Delisting of Lesser Prairie-Chicken
sections.text: Part A says that no matter what any other law says, the lesser prairie-chicken bird (a type of prairie bird with the scientific name Tympanuchus pallidicinctus) must be removed from the government's lists of threatened and endangered species. This includes all different groups of these birds. These lists are created under the Endangered Species Act of nineteen seventy three, which is a federal law that protects animals and plants that might go extinct. Part B changes the Endangered Species Act itself. It adds new language saying that the Secretary (the government official in charge of this law) is not allowed to decide that the lesser prairie-chicken is either an endangered species or a threatened species. An endangered species is one that might completely disappear, and a threatened species is one that could become endangered. This change means the lesser prairie-chicken can never be put back on these protection lists in the future.
